label: Lung
quick_take: The lung-cancer trajectory in CI5 reads like the history of the cigarette itself. Men peaked and turned down almost everywhere between vol V–VIII (1980s–90s). Women are still rising *everywhere* except a handful of early-quit leaders (USA, Canada anglophone, UK). The biggest absolute outlier remains the Canadian/New-Zealand Indigenous women, not East Asia.

findings.title: Women peak (almost) nowhere yet
findings.text: The "biggest female decreases" panel is empty. Canada Saskatchewan, Quebec, New Brunswick, Norway and others show female ASR multiplying 7–9× since vol I. Female lung cancer is the single most universally-rising indicator in the dataset.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Women peak (almost) nowhere yet

findings.title: Canadian Nunavut women, vol XI: ASR 97.5
findings.text: 4.8× the world median, based on 41 cases in a single volume. New Zealand women 229→5549 cases (NZ-aggregate ASR 6.98→24.81, +255 %) confirm the broader Indigenous-heavy-country pattern. Canadian NWT women 72 (vol VIII). The Indigenous female lung-cancer burden is higher than almost any male burden anywhere today.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Canadian Nunavut women, vol XI: ASR 97.5

findings.title: The male Finland inverted-U is textbook
findings.text: 83.3 (vol I) → 100.8 peak (vol III) → 28.9 (vol XII), a 65 % fall from peak. USA White San Francisco men peaked at 86.8 (vol IV) and fell to 22.5 (vol XII), a 72 % fall from peak. The US Georgia Atlanta White men peaked at 92 and fell to 32.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — The male Finland inverted-U is textbook

findings.title: Polish regions have the world's highest never-declined male levels
findings.text: Warsaw City men 94 (vol IV), Cracow 101 (vol IV), Warsaw Rural 65 (vol VI), peaks in the mid-1970s. Decline has been slower than in most other European registries.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Polish regions have the world's highest never-declined male levels

findings.title: US Black-vs-Native male ratio 12× in vol IV and still 11× in vol XII
findings.text: Among women, Black-vs-Native ratio peaked at 14× in vol V. Much more persistent than comparable gradients for tobacco-related cancers.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — US Black-vs-Native male ratio 12× in vol IV and still 11× in vol XII

findings.title: Extreme male:female ratios still exist in Muslim-majority registries
findings.text: Turkey Edirne 15.5:1, Belarus 14:1, Mali 19:1, Tunisia Centre 21:1. These are the *least*-converged male:female ratios for lung cancer left in the world.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Extreme male:female ratios still exist in Muslim-majority registries

findings.title: cancer_warning flags this site
findings.text: ICD-7 was used for vols I–III, with some definitional discrepancies with later volumes. Don't place too much weight on the exact vol I–II levels — directionally they are fine but the magnitude should not be over-parsed. All lung findings above that span this break (notably Finland vol I, Canadian Saskatchewan vol I) inherit this caveat.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — cancer_warning flags this site

findings.text: US bimodal "age-curves" in the scratch output reflect population-scale registries where ascertainment slightly differs — probably not a biological bimodality.
A single figure showing "women reaching men's historical peak" across 6 northern registries would be striking and publishable.
The Indigenous (Nunavut / NWT / Māori / Alaska Native) female lung-cancer excess is in need of a dedicated review piece.
Polish men have declined slowest in Europe — a focused analysis against smoking-prevalence and tobacco-control policy timing would inform ongoing debate.
